metadata.section: Z - Transform \quad (\text{applicable for discrete-time intervals only})\\

text:
! 
→ Depending on the value of \( T_0 \), the output may converge (or not).

→ The Z-transform is exactly equal to the Fourier Transform (FT), where \( c \) is replaced by \( z^k \) instead of \( j\omega \), which we replace with \( e^{j\omega} \):
\[
X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}
\]

Given \( x(n) \), we have:
\[
X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n} = Z(x(n))
\]

The relationship is expressed as:
\( x(n) \leftrightarrow Z \leftrightarrow X(z) \)

→ If the FT exists, then it must be:
\[
\mathcal{F}\{x(n)\} = Z\{[x(n)]\}, \, z = e^{j\omega}
\]

The subject representing the \( z \)-plane is:
\[ 
z = re^{j\theta} 
\]
- \( |z| \) intersects \( -\text{Re} z \).

→ The most important line is \( |z| = 1 \), which describes a circle (radius = 1):
\[
\frac{1}{|z|} \text{ (unit circle) }
\]

metadata.section: → Depending on the value of \( T_0 \), the output may converge (or not).
